items.subject: music
items.topic: how recording technology changed the way music was composed
items.transcript:
Today I want to talk about how recording technology gradually transformed the way composers actually wrote music. Before the late nineteenth century, almost all composition was prepared for live performance only. A piece was finished when the ink dried on the manuscript, and the only feedback the composer received came from rehearsals and the eventual audience.

Things began to change with Edison's phonograph in 1877. For the first time a sound could be captured, played back, and listened to again. Initially, composers treated recordings simply as documentation, a way to preserve a performance. But within two decades, several began to think about the recording itself as a medium with its own constraints. Early discs could hold only about three minutes of music, so popular songwriters compressed verses and shortened instrumental introductions to fit. The three-minute pop song that dominated the twentieth century was, in part, a side effect of disc length.

The larger shift came with magnetic tape after the Second World War. Tape allowed editing: splicing two takes together, removing wrong notes, repeating a single phrase many times to layer it. Composers like Pierre Schaeffer treated tape as a compositional instrument in itself, building entire pieces from manipulated recorded sounds rather than written notation.

By the 1960s, popular musicians had embraced the same approach. Multitrack recording let producers and composers build a song one layer at a time, and the recording studio effectively became the score. What I want you to notice is this: technology did not just preserve composition; it gradually changed what composition was. A piece existed not on paper but on tape.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: How recording technology gradually transformed the process of musical composition
options.B: The three-minute pop song as the most important format of the twentieth century
options.C: Edison's invention of the phonograph and its commercial success
options.D: The training of composers in early twentieth-century European conservatories

questions.explanation: 讲座中心是录音技术如何逐步改变作曲过程：从单纯保存声音到唱片限时影响歌曲结构，再到磁带剪辑和多轨录音让录音棚本身成为乐谱。A 概括了这一主线。B 只覆盖一个段落细节，too_narrow。C 把背景信息当作主旨，wrong_topic。D 完全没有出现在讲座中，wrong_topic。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what effect did the length limit of early discs have on popular songwriting?

options.B: Songwriters compressed verses and shortened introductions to fit
options.C: Performers refused to make commercial recordings
options.D: Discs were lengthened to match standard song formats

questions.explanation: 教授明确指出早期唱片只能容纳约三分钟音乐，所以词曲作家压缩段落、缩短前奏。B 与原文一致。A 与所述相反，reversed_logic。C 没有依据。D 因果颠倒：是歌曲格式被唱片长度塑造，而不是相反。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what did Pierre Schaeffer treat magnetic tape as?

options.B: A replacement for the early disc format
options.D: A scientific tool for studying acoustics

questions.explanation: 原文明说 Schaeffer 把磁带当作一种作曲乐器来使用，用录制和操控的声音构建作品。C 正确。A 把媒介商业用途和创作工具混淆，topic_adjacent。B 没有提到 Schaeffer 与唱片竞争，wrong_detail。D 没有任何科学声学的提法，wrong_topic。

questions.stem: What does the professor imply about a popular song produced in the 1960s using multitrack recording?

options.A: It was usually still written down as a complete score before any recording took place
options.B: It was technically inferior to recordings made on early Edison discs
options.C: It existed primarily as a written manuscript stored in studio archives
options.D: Its final form effectively existed on the tape rather than in any written notation

questions.explanation: 教授结尾强调录音棚本身成为乐谱，作品不再依赖纸上的乐谱而存在于磁带上。D 正是这一暗示。A 与教授的论点相反，reversed_logic。B 是无依据的价值判断，over_generalization。C 与多轨录音逻辑相悖，topic_adjacent。

items.subject: geology
items.topic: the role of plate tectonics in forming mountain ranges
items.transcript:
Today I want to talk about how mountain ranges are formed, and specifically the role of plate tectonics in that process. The Earth's outer shell is broken into about a dozen large plates that drift slowly across the planet's surface. Most major mountain ranges sit precisely where two of these plates have collided or pushed past each other.

The clearest example is the Himalayas. About fifty million years ago, the plate carrying India crashed into the Asian plate. Neither plate could sink under the other easily, because both pieces of continental crust were too thick and too low in density. So instead, the rocks at the boundary were shoved upward, folded, and stacked. The mountains rose, and they're still rising today by about five millimeters per year.

Not all mountain ranges form in the same way. Along the western edge of South America, an oceanic plate is being forced beneath the continent. The ocean plate is denser, so it slides downward into the Earth's interior, but in the process it scrapes off sediments and triggers volcanic activity above. That's how the Andes formed: partly through compression, but mostly through this long, slow descent of an oceanic plate.

There's a third pattern, too. The Mid-Atlantic Ridge is also a mountain range, but it sits underwater. There, two oceanic plates are moving apart, and molten rock pushes up through the gap to harden into new crust.

So the takeaway is this: collisions, descents, and separations all produce mountains, but the shapes, heights, and rock types depend strongly on which kind of tectonic boundary is involved.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: Why the Himalayas continue to rise at five millimeters per year
options.B: How different types of plate boundaries produce different mountain ranges
options.C: The danger of volcanic eruptions along the western edge of South America
options.D: The chemistry of molten rock at the Mid-Atlantic Ridge

questions.explanation: 讲座主线是不同板块边界（碰撞、俯冲、张裂）如何形成不同的山脉。B 是对全文的合理概括。A 只覆盖一个例子，too_narrow。C 仅是顺带提及的火山，too_narrow。D 是次要细节，too_narrow。

questions.stem: According to the professor, why do neither plate sinks under the other where the Himalayas form?

options.A: Because the rocks at the boundary are heavier than oceanic plates
options.B: Because volcanic activity prevents subduction at the boundary
options.C: Because both pieces of continental crust are too thick and low in density
options.D: Because the boundary has been moving in opposite directions for fifty million years

questions.explanation: 教授明确说两块大陆地壳都过厚、密度过低，因此谁也无法下潜。C 正确。A 与原因相反，reversed_logic。B 在喜马拉雅段落根本没提火山，wrong_detail。D 把方向问题误当成原因，topic_adjacent。

questions.stem: Why does the professor mention the Mid-Atlantic Ridge?

options.A: To argue that ocean ridges produce taller mountains than continental ranges
options.B: To suggest that all mountain ranges originate under the ocean
options.C: To recall an example already discussed in a previous class
options.D: To illustrate a third mountain-forming process: two plates moving apart

questions.explanation: 教授引入大西洋洋中脊作为第三种模式（板块张裂）的例子，与前两种碰撞、俯冲并列。D 正确。A 是没有依据的价值比较，over_inference。B 是过度概括，over_inference。C 没有这种课堂回顾迹象，wrong_purpose。

questions.stem: What does the professor imply about the Andes?

options.A: Their rock types and shape reflect a subduction zone rather than a continental collision
options.B: They are growing at a faster rate than the Himalayas
options.C: They are formed mainly by two oceanic plates moving apart
options.D: They will eventually become as tall as the Himalayas

questions.explanation: 教授把安第斯归为大洋板块下潜并伴有火山的类型，与喜马拉雅式的两块大陆地壳碰撞不同。A 正确推断。B 没有比较增长率，over_inference。C 把安第斯误归入张裂型，reversed_logic。D 是无依据的未来预测，over_inference。

items.subject: anthropology
items.topic: the anthropology of food taboos across societies
items.transcript:
Today let's look at food taboos, and try to understand why so many human societies tell their members that certain foods are not to be eaten. From an anthropologist's perspective, taboos are interesting precisely because they often look irrational from outside the culture, and yet they're sustained over many generations.

The oldest scholarly explanation, going back to the nineteenth century, treated taboos as superstition. Outside observers concluded that the people involved simply did not understand what was safe to eat. That explanation has fallen out of favor for a fairly obvious reason: many tabooed foods are clearly nutritious and well-known, so ignorance cannot be the answer.

A second approach, popular with mid-twentieth-century anthropologists, is the functional or ecological reading. On this view, taboos quietly conserve scarce or fragile resources. A community that forbids hunting a particular bird during nesting season prevents the population from collapsing. The taboo doesn't have to be explained in ecological terms by the people who follow it; the rule itself does the work. This kind of explanation has its limits, though, because not every taboo corresponds neatly to a real ecological pressure.

A third, more recent perspective treats taboos as markers of group identity. Eating, or refusing to eat, particular foods becomes a daily, visible signal of belonging. The taboo is meaningful because it distinguishes us from outsiders, and the everyday discipline of following it strengthens commitment to a community.

Most contemporary anthropologists now combine these approaches. A given taboo may have multiple layers — partly ecological, partly social, partly historical — and the relative weight of each layer changes from case to case.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: Why nineteenth-century anthropologists dismissed food taboos as mere superstition
options.B: Whether food taboos prevent over-hunting of fragile bird populations
options.C: How anthropological thinking about food taboos has shifted across competing explanations
options.D: Why eating habits are the strongest available marker of cultural identity

questions.explanation: 讲座主线是回顾人类学对食物禁忌解释的演变：迷信论、生态/功能论、身份论以及当代综合视角。C 概括正确。A 只是第一种解释，too_narrow。B 仅是举例，too_narrow。D 是第三种解释的过度泛化，over_generalization。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what is the main limitation of the functional/ecological explanation?

options.A: It does not allow anthropologists to compare societies
options.B: It depends on ideas that have been entirely discredited since 1900
options.C: It works only for taboos involving plant foods, not animals
options.D: Not every taboo corresponds neatly to a real ecological pressure

questions.explanation: 教授明确指出，并非每个禁忌都能对应真实的生态压力，这是该解释的局限。D 正确。A 完全没有依据。B 把第一种解释的过时与第二种混淆，wrong_detail。C 没有植物 vs 动物的区分，wrong_detail。

questions.stem: Why does the professor mention that taboos are sustained over many generations?

options.A: To highlight why anthropologists find taboos a particularly puzzling phenomenon worth explaining
options.B: To prove that older societies were generally more disciplined than modern societies
options.C: To argue that food taboos are likely to disappear in industrialized economies
options.D: To suggest that ecological explanations are the only adequate account of taboos

questions.explanation: 教授提出多世代延续这一现象，是为了说明禁忌从外部看似乎不合理却长期存在，正因如此值得人类学家解释。A 正确。B 是没有依据的价值判断，over_inference。C 与讲座立场相反，wrong_purpose。D 与讲座的多元综合立场相反，wrong_purpose。

questions.stem: What does the professor imply about how anthropologists today typically explain a particular taboo?

options.A: They usually accept the functional/ecological explanation without further analysis
options.B: They generally consider several factors and weight them differently from case to case
options.C: They prefer to interpret taboos primarily as misunderstandings of nutrition
options.D: They mostly view taboos as transient phenomena that disappear over a single generation

questions.explanation: 教授在结尾说当代人类学家通常综合多种因素，并根据具体禁忌调整权重。B 正是这一含义。A 与综合立场相反，reversed_logic。C 重复了被弃用的迷信解释，wrong_detail。D 与禁忌长期延续的事实相反，reversed_logic。

items.subject: physics
items.topic: the principle behind how a lever multiplies force
items.transcript:
Today I want to look at one of the oldest devices in physics, the lever, and explain why it lets a small force lift a much heavier load. The principle goes back to Archimedes and is sometimes called the law of the lever.

A lever is just a rigid bar that turns around a fixed point called the fulcrum. On one side you apply an input force; on the other side a load resists you. The crucial idea is that what matters isn't simply how hard you push, but how far you are from the fulcrum. The further you sit from the fulcrum, the larger the rotating effect of your push. Engineers call this rotating effect a moment, or a torque.

For the lever to balance, the moment on the load side and the moment on your side must be equal. That means if the load is sitting twice as close to the fulcrum as you are, your input force only needs to be half the weight of the load. Move the fulcrum closer to the load, and the multiplication grows further.

There is, however, a trade-off. You pay for the multiplied force in distance. When the load rises a small height, your end of the lever travels a much greater distance. So total energy is conserved — force is multiplied, but the path of your hand grows in exactly the same proportion.

This principle is everywhere. A crowbar pulls out a nail because you sit far from the contact point. Scissors cut paper because the blade meets the paper close to the fulcrum. Even bones and muscles work the same way: many joints in the human body are levers, with the fulcrum at the joint itself.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: How Archimedes discovered the principle of buoyancy
options.B: Why crowbars are more useful for daily work than scissors are
options.C: How human bones evolved into highly efficient lever systems
options.D: How a lever multiplies force, and the distance trade-off that accompanies it

questions.explanation: 讲座主线是杠杆放大力的原理以及伴随的距离代价。D 正确。A 把杠杆原理混淆为浮力原理，wrong_topic。B 是举例之一的价值化判断，too_narrow。C 是结尾的一个例子，too_narrow。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what determines the rotating effect of a force on a lever?

options.A: Both the size of the force and the distance from the fulcrum
options.B: The mass of the rigid bar itself
options.C: The angle at which the load is attached
options.D: The total height the load needs to be lifted

questions.explanation: 教授强调力的旋转效果（力矩）由力的大小和距离支点的距离共同决定。A 正确。B、C、D 在讲座中都没有作为力矩决定因素出现，都是 wrong_detail 或 topic_adjacent。

questions.stem: Why does the professor mention that the input end of the lever travels a much greater distance?

options.A: To remind students that levers only function when they are perfectly balanced
options.B: To make the point that total energy is conserved — the force gained is paid for in distance
options.C: To argue that long levers are usually preferred to short ones in modern engineering
options.D: To suggest that moving the fulcrum has no effect on the input force required

questions.explanation: 教授提到行程更长是为了说明能量守恒——放大的力以更长的行程为代价。B 正确。A 偏离杠杆的功用，wrong_purpose。C 没有此类价值判断，over_inference。D 与原文相反，literal_restatement 但是颠倒。

questions.stem: Why does the professor mention scissors and bones at the end of the lecture?

options.A: To suggest that the lever principle is gradually being replaced by newer mechanisms
options.B: To complain that schools rarely teach simple machines anymore
options.C: To illustrate that the same lever principle applies across many familiar examples
options.D: To warn students that not every human joint actually behaves like a lever

questions.explanation: 结尾的剪刀和人体骨骼都是杠杆原理普遍适用的例证。C 正确。A 与论旨相反，wrong_purpose。B 是离题的抱怨，wrong_purpose。D 与教授所说骨骼按杠杆原理工作相反，reversed_logic。
